content.text: Approved Claude Model Version Matrix

Not all Claude model versions are appropriate for all production use cases at a given point in time. As Anthropic releases new model versions, older versions move through a lifecycle from current to supported to maintenance to deprecated. An approved model version matrix codifies which versions are eligible for new integrations, which are permitted for existing integrations, and which require migration. Without a formal matrix, teams make ad hoc version decisions, leading to inconsistent model versions across services and delayed migrations when versions approach end of life.

attrs.alt: Claude model version lifecycle matrix showing Current, Supported, Maintenance, and Deprecated status for model families with production eligibility and patch support columns

content.text: The Four Lifecycle Stages
Each model version passes through four lifecycle stages. The stage determines what operations are permitted with that version and what obligations exist for teams using it.

The eligible_for_new_integrations field is the most operationally significant: it is the gate that prevents a new microservice from being built on a MAINTENANCE model that will require migration in 12 months. Code review and integration review processes should check the approved model version matrix at the point of model selection, not after the service is deployed to production.
Model Version Selection for New Integrations

When building a new Claude API integration, the model version selection follows a decision process based on use case requirements rather than defaulting to whatever model is currently latest. Different model families serve different needs: Sonnet-family models balance capability with cost for general-purpose text generation; Opus-family models are suited to complex multi-step reasoning; Haiku-family models prioritise low latency for high-throughput applications. The approved matrix constrains the choice to CURRENT models within the appropriate family.
Use Case Pattern
Recommended Family
Key Constraint
General text generation, summarisation
claude-3-5-sonnet (CURRENT)
Must be CURRENT status in approved matrix
Complex multi-step reasoning, agentic tasks
claude-3-5-sonnet or claude-3-opus
Opus eligible while SUPPORTED; evaluate cost vs capability
High-throughput, latency-sensitive
claude-3-5-haiku (CURRENT)
Must be CURRENT; do not use older Haiku versions
Batch processing, cost-optimised
claude-3-5-haiku (CURRENT)
Verify CURRENT status before integration
Tool use / function calling
claude-3-5-sonnet (CURRENT)
Verify tool_use block format unchanged in version
Maintaining the Approved Version Matrix

The approved version matrix is a living document. It must be updated when Anthropic releases new model versions, when versions transition between lifecycle stages, and when end-of-life dates are announced. The team that owns the matrix has two responsibilities: keeping the matrix current and broadcasting lifecycle stage changes to all teams using affected versions.

The requires_migration_ticket method connects the matrix update process to the dependency upgrade program: when a version transitions to MAINTENANCE or DEPRECATED, migration tickets are automatically filed for every service in the affected_services list. These tickets carry the migration_deadline as a due date and the migration_target model as the recommended destination. The upgrade program's scheduled window mechanism then handles the actual migration execution.
Version Pinning Policy

The approved matrix defines the permitted model IDs. The version pinning policy defines how those IDs must be specified in application code and configuration. Two approaches exist: pinning to exact model IDs (claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022) or using date-tagged latest aliases (claude-3-5-sonnet-latest). The policy decision has operational consequences for how model version changes propagate to the application.
Exact ID pinning (recommended for production):

The application always uses a specific, known model version. New model versions do not affect the application until explicitly upgraded. The downside is that the application must be actively upgraded to receive improvements; it will not benefit from silent model improvements automatically.
Alias pinning (use with caution):

The application uses an alias like claude-3-5-sonnet-latest that Anthropic updates when a new version of that model family is released. The application receives improvements without a code change, but may also receive behaviour changes without advance notice. Alias pinning is acceptable only when the application is resilient to output variation and has automated golden-set monitoring in production.
Configuration over code:

The model ID should be a configuration value, not a string literal in application code. A configuration-driven model ID can be updated without a code deploy — important for rapid rollbacks when a model version change causes a regression. The configuration value is still governed by the approved matrix; production configuration changes require the same approval as code changes.
Matrix compliance at deploy time:

The deployment pipeline should validate that the configured model ID appears in the approved matrix at CURRENT or SUPPORTED status before deploying to production. A deploy that would put a DEPRECATED model version into production should be blocked by the pipeline, not caught post-deploy.
Handling the Transition from Supported to Maintenance

The SUPPORTED-to-MAINTENANCE transition is the most operationally significant stage change. It means the model version is no longer recommended for new integrations and existing integrations have a 12-month migration window. Teams must respond in two ways: blocking new integrations in architecture review, and filing and prioritising migration tickets for existing integrations.

The is_overdue_to_start method creates an early warning system: even if the migration deadline is months away, a service that has passed its migration_sprint_start date without beginning migration work is already falling behind schedule. Monitoring this field across all services in MAINTENANCE status gives the platform team a real-time view of migration programme health, not just which services have met or missed the final deadline.
Enforcing Matrix Compliance Across Services

Publishing a model version matrix is only useful if the matrix is actually consulted when decisions are made. Without enforcement, teams will ignore it under time pressure, choose deprecated versions for compatibility reasons, or simply not know the matrix exists. Enforcement operates at three points: architecture review (when a new service proposes its model choice), deployment pipeline (when code with a model ID reaches production), and periodic audit (when existing services are checked for compliance).

The blocks_deploy method is the integration point for CI/CD pipelines. A pre-deploy step reads the configured model ID from environment variables or configuration files, instantiates a ModelComplianceCheck with the current approved matrix, and calls blocks_deploy(). If it returns True, the deployment is halted with the compliance message. This prevents the situation where a developer pins to a deprecated model in a hurry and the problem is only discovered in a post-deploy audit.
Managing Multi-Version Environments

Large organisations running multiple Claude-powered services will inevitably have different services on different model versions at any given time. Some services may be on the CURRENT Sonnet version while others are on SUPPORTED Opus or still migrating from MAINTENANCE. This creates a multi-version environment where the organisation has explicit positions on multiple model versions simultaneously. The approved matrix helps manage this by making each service's position visible and giving each service a clear path to the next milestone.

The compliance_summary method produces a dashboard-ready snapshot. Organisations should track these metrics quarterly: compliance_rate (percentage of services not on deprecated versions), deprecated_count (must be driven to zero), and at_risk_60d (services approaching migration deadline — a leading indicator of future compliance problems). A compliance_rate of 1.0 with no deprecated services and no at_risk services is the target state.
Cost Implications of Model Version Choices

Model version choices have direct cost implications. Different Claude model versions have different pricing per million input and output tokens, and different capability-to-cost ratios. The approved matrix's recommendation of CURRENT versions is partly a capability recommendation, but for cost-optimised use cases, the right version may be a CURRENT Haiku rather than a CURRENT Sonnet. The matrix governs eligibility; cost optimisation governs selection within the eligible set.
Decision Factor
Matrix Role
Team Role
Version eligibility
Defines the permitted set
Must pick from permitted set only
Capability vs cost trade-off
Not in scope — matrix is a compliance tool
Team evaluates within permitted set
Security patch coverage
Documented per stage
Must use versions with active patch coverage
Model behaviour / output quality
Not in scope — matrix is a lifecycle tool
Golden-set evaluation governs quality gates
Migration timing
Sets deadlines via stage definitions
Plans and executes migration within deadline

This separation of concerns is deliberate. The approved matrix answers "are you allowed to use this version?" — it does not answer "which permitted version should you use?" That second question is answered by the team's own capability and cost evaluation. Keeping the matrix scoped to lifecycle compliance prevents it from becoming an unwieldy document that tries to encode every possible model selection rationale.
Communicating Model Version Changes to API Consumers

When the organisation operates a platform that exposes Claude capabilities to downstream API consumers (internal teams or external customers), model version changes require consumer communication in addition to internal matrix updates. A downstream consumer who has built their application against a specific model's behaviour patterns needs advance notice when the platform will transition to a different model version — even if that transition is from SUPPORTED to a newer CURRENT version that improves output quality.
Version stability guarantees:

Document which model version identifiers are stable (an exact pinned ID will never change its behaviour) and which are dynamic (a latest alias will be updated as new versions become current). Consumers who need stability must use pinned IDs; consumers who accept variation in exchange for automatic improvements may use aliases.
Advance notice windows:

When a platform-level default model version will change (e.g., the platform upgrades from claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 to a newer Sonnet release as the default), provide at least 30 days advance notice in the API changelog. Consumers building production applications need time to run their own evaluations and adjust their integrations.
Deprecated model version sunset dates:

When a model version in the approved matrix reaches DEPRECATED status and an API endpoint will stop accepting calls to that model ID, communicate the sunset date a minimum of 90 days in advance. Consumers must know the hard cutoff so they can plan migrations against a real deadline rather than an open-ended recommendation.
Migration guides with concrete examples:

Advance notice of a model version deprecation must be accompanied by a migration guide that shows the consumer exactly what to change (typically just the model ID string, but sometimes system prompt adjustments if the new model processes them differently). Consumers should not have to discover the migration path through trial and error.
Summary

The approved model version matrix assigns each Claude model version a lifecycle stage — CURRENT, SUPPORTED, MAINTENANCE, or DEPRECATED — that governs its production eligibility and patch coverage. CURRENT versions are recommended for all new integrations. SUPPORTED versions are production-eligible but receive no new features. MAINTENANCE versions block new integrations and require migration within 12 months. DEPRECATED versions require immediate migration and receive no security patches. The matrix is updated when Anthropic releases new versions or announces stage transitions, with automated notifications to affected services and migration ticket creation for MAINTENANCE and DEPRECATED transitions. Version pinning policy requires model IDs to be configuration values rather than code literals, enabling rapid rollbacks, and recommends exact ID pinning for production (alias pinning only for applications with automated golden-set monitoring). Deployment pipelines validate matrix compliance at deploy time, blocking production deploys that reference DEPRECATED model versions.
